entries.title: Strategic Design
entries.content:
Error: include
-----
Cannot call function include(MutableContext context, String path, optional ContextSandbox sandbox) with arguments (./_setup.qd): 
File c:\Users\nickg\Documents\University\Web\CrowdVision\documentation\developer\domain\.\_setup.qd does not exist.

	.include {.pathtoroot/_setup.qd}

Strategic Design

CrowdVision is modelled with a Domain-Driven Design (DDD) mindset. The system is not a single monolithic model; it is a set of collaborating models, each owned by a service and each speaking its own dialect of a shared Ubiquitous Language. This page describes the strategic layer — the subdomains, the bounded contexts, and the relationships between them. The tactical layer (aggregates, entities, value objects, and domain services) is documented per context on the following pages.

A pragmatic stance
-----
DDD is used here as a design compass, not a rulebook. The strategic boundaries below are firm and have proven stable. The tactical patterns are applied where they earn their keep: aggregates and value objects are expressed as Mongoose subdocuments and typed structs rather than a formal domain layer, repositories are realised implicitly through the Service and Model layers, and domain events are carried over Redis pub/sub messages. Some boundaries were intentionally left soft because the product ideas were still maturing. Where the implementation diverges from textbook DDD, the per-context pages call it out explicitly.

-----

Domain and Subdomains

The overall domain is real-time monitoring of physical spaces through a digital twin. We classify the subdomains by how much competitive value they carry, which in turn drives how much modelling effort each receives.

Spatial Digital TwinTelemetry Ingestion & ThresholdsTelemetry DistributionIdentity & AccessAlertingKnowledge & AssistanceReal-Time TransportSubdomainCoreCoreCoreSupportingSupportingSupportingGenericTypetwin-servicesensor-servicecontracts-serviceauth-servicenotification-serviceagent-servicesocket-serviceOwning ServiceThe 3D building model is the product’s differentiator and receives the richest model.Turning raw sensor signals into meaningful, bounded readings is central business logic.Per-building stream shaping is what makes the live dashboard feel tailored.Multi-tenant access control is essential but not a differentiator.Push alerting supports the core experience without defining it.The LLM assistant augments the product; it is explicitly experimental.A commodity WebSocket relay with no domain model of its own.Rationale

-----

Bounded Contexts

Each core and supporting subdomain is realised as exactly one bounded context, and each bounded context maps one-to-one onto a deployable service with a private database. This alignment is deliberate: a context boundary is also a network and persistence boundary, so a model can never silently leak into another context through a shared table.



The Real-Time Transport context (socket-service) is omitted from the context map: it carries messages between contexts but holds no model and enforces no invariants.

-----

Context Map

The relationships between contexts are described with the standard DDD integration patterns. They tell a new contributor who depends on whom and which side is allowed to dictate the contract.

Identity → all other contextsBuilding Management → Telemetry IngestionBuilding Management → Telemetry DistributionTelemetry Ingestion → Telemetry DistributionBuilding Management → AlertingBuilding Management → Knowledge & AssistanceUpstream → DownstreamPublished LanguageCustomer / SupplierCustomer / SupplierOpen Host Service + Published LanguageCustomer / Supplier (Anti-Corruption)Anti-Corruption LayerPatternThe signed JWT payload (memberships, roles, domains) is the published language every context conforms to. Downstream contexts are Conformists: they verify and read the token but never reshape it.On building creation the twin pushes a threshold clone (PUT /thresholds/buildings/:id). The twin is the customer driving the contract.The twin seeds default column preferences (POST /preferences/init/:id) and exposes its own structural metrics (roomName, roomMaxOccupancy) as part of the metric catalog.The sensor service publishes a self-describing Metric Contract at GET /contracts. The contracts service discovers and aggregates these, so new metric types appear without code changes downstream.Alerting resolves a building’s domains by calling the twin (GET /twin/domain/:buildingId) and translates the response into its own subscription concepts.The agent reaches live building data only through read-only tools that wrap the twin API, keeping the twin model out of the agent’s domain.Integration

-----

The Shared Identifier Convention

Across the entire platform, a Domain is referenced by its name string, never by a database identifier. This is the closest thing CrowdVision has to a Shared Kernel, but it is deliberately minimal: a single, stable, human-readable string rather than a shared code library.



Because the domain name travels inside the JWT and is stored verbatim in every context’s database, multi-tenant isolation is enforced symmetrically and without any cross-database joins or synchronous service-to-service calls on the hot path. Each context answers “may this caller see this data?” entirely from data it already owns.

-----

Tactical Patterns at a Glance

The building blocks below recur throughout the per-context pages. Their CrowdVision implementations are listed so the mapping from theory to code is unambiguous.

Aggregate RootEntityValue ObjectDomain ServiceRepositoryDomain EventDDD Building BlockA top-level Mongoose model (or Rust struct) that owns a consistency boundary, e.g. Account, Building, BuildingThreshold.An object with identity that lives inside an aggregate, e.g. Room within Building.An immutable, identity-less subdocument, e.g. Coordinates, Dimensions, SubscriptionPreference.Stateless logic that does not belong to a single entity, e.g. tokenService, totpService, the sensor SensorKernel.Not a dedicated class; the Service + Model layers together play the repository role (see The Microservices.A message published to Redis, e.g. telemetry:raw, telemetry:filtered:{building}, notifications.How it is realised in CrowdVision

Where the model is intentionally anemic
-----
Several aggregates are persistence-shaped rather than behaviour-rich — the schema carries the data and the Service layer carries the rules. This was a conscious trade-off for development speed while the bounded contexts were still settling. The pages that follow flag the spots where behaviour should eventually migrate back onto the aggregate.
